title: What 1ml of Filler Actually Looks Like - Correcting Overtreated Lips
filename: What 1ml of Filler Actually Looks Like - Correcting Overtreated Lips.en.vtt
excerpt: In this video, I'm going to show you what 1 mil of filler looks like on a patient's lips. It's actually surprisingly easy to ruin someone's lips with 1 mil of filler. So, we need to understand what it looks like in that context.

segments.text: In this video, I'm going to show you what 1 mil of filler looks like on a patient's lips. It's actually surprisingly easy to ruin someone's lips with 1 mil of filler. So, we need to understand what it looks like in that context. The scale of the lips to the volume of the filler really matter. And this is particularly important with small lips. Small lips are easiest to lose the shape and detail. In your effort to achieve proportion, you lose some of the most important aspects of beauty. Let's have a look at what a typical procedure in terms of volume might look like on some smaller than average lips. This is 0.05 ms at the GK point. Suddenly doesn't look so small anymore. Now I'll do a linear thread of 0.05 ms tracking outwards from the GK point to enhance the vermillion border followed by a vertical injection which I might use to invert the lip to try and get more of the pink part of the lip to show. Two linear threads of 0.05 05 would be quite a low volume for many clinicians. But look already at how much of the lip it is taking up. Let's now do

the lateral aspect of the million border and the lateral aspect of the tubicle each with 0.05 ms. I'm already feeling at this stage there isn't really a lot of room left, is there? 0.05 mil bololis, 0.05 mil as a linear thread, 0.05 05 ms in a vertical injection followed by another 0.05 ms in a second vertical injection. Lateral lip 0.05 ms. Let's see what a common lower lip treatment would look like with the average volumes. 0.05 mil on the lower lip angle followed by 0.05 05 ms laterally to create that central dominance followed by 0.05 mil in the tubicle. Gosh, those boluses look way bigger than I thought they would. Let's have a look

at the next side of the lip. Repeating the same process. 0.05 ms linear thread. Also very interesting to note how the filler flows so directionally out of the bevel of the needle. It comes out almost away from the needle upwards from the bevel, which is useful information. 0.05 mil across the middle portion of the lips each linear thread. For any injector, you might look at this and think that all looks a little bit messy. That makes me feel a little bit less confident about putting higher volumes in because it is flowing so easily and covering so much of the lip. I still haven't used a full mill, though, which is why I'm going with a couple more vertical injections. As you can see, I'm really struggling now for room to find areas to put the filler on. And it really changes my perception on how much product you can actually put, particularly in smaller lips. In the next part of this video,

I'm smoothing it over like I would do with a patient who had a lip injection. I might gently massage the lips to check there are no irregularities, lumps, or bumps. It's important for injectors to understand their volumes, particularly when dealing with smaller lips. The reason for this is because the difference you make is proportionately bigger when lips are smaller. And that means it's easier to create an overtreated look even if you're using less volume. What does overtreated mean? It means an unnatural looking result. What is it that makes people look unnatural? For one thing, it is a loss of natural shape. If you think about smaller lips as having a natural shape and you add more volume, the more you add, the more it'll distort the natural shape and they tend to have limiting factors that are different in different areas of the lip. For example, a simultaneous injection all the way along the lip. You will find it harder to lift the middle proportion than you will the lateral portion, which means you get more sausage shaped lips at the end if you do a harmonious similar volume all the way along the lip. Many clinicians will fear this and notice halfway through a treatment that they've

actually unfortunately made this problem a reality. So, what would you do during a procedure if this were to occur? The first thing is filler that's just gone in is usually easier to massage. So, you can often make an adjustment during the procedure. You can also shape the lips with a sculpting squeeze so that the tubicles dominate better, which is essentially doing the same thing. You're squeezing filler away from the lateral injections towards the medial so that the mid part of the lip dominates. Just remember that during the procedure, swelling is a big part of what you see. I think up to 80% in some cases is just the swelling from the procedure rather than the location of the filler. So, whenever consulting your patients and assessing them during the procedure, remember to take into account swelling. You also got risk uh risk of infections, reactions, redness, bruising, swelling.
